content.text: Semantic Kernel (SK) is Microsoft's open-source AI orchestration SDK for Python, C#, and Java. It lets you compose AI capabilities — LLM calls, code functions, and memory — into "plugins" that a central
object can invoke and chain. Unlike LangChain's chain metaphor or LlamaIndex's document focus, SK is structured around the plugin-as-skill model familiar from Microsoft 365 Copilot's architecture.
the hub that holds LLM services, memory, plugins, and configuration
a single callable unit — either a prompt template (semantic function) or plain Python (native function)
the typed dict passed between functions in a pipeline

# pip install semantic-kernel
import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(
    OpenAIChatCompletion(
        service_id="chat",
        ai_model_id="gpt-4o-mini",
        # api_key read from OPENAI_API_KEY env by default
    )
)
print("Kernel ready:", kernel)
Semantic Kernel is the AI SDK powering Microsoft 365 Copilot's plugin ecosystem. The same plugin model works in your own apps.
A semantic function wraps a prompt template in a
syntax. The function renders the prompt, calls the LLM service, and returns a

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.prompt_template import PromptTemplateConfig

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o-mini"))

# Define a semantic function inline
summarise_fn = kernel.add_function(
    plugin_name="TextUtils",
    function_name="Summarise",
    prompt="Summarise the following in {{$max_sentences}} sentences:\n{{$input}}",
    description="Summarises a passage of text.",
)

async def main():
    from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory
    from semantic_kernel.kernel_pydantic import KernelBaseModel

    result = await kernel.invoke(
        summarise_fn,
        input="Semantic Kernel is an open-source SDK that lets you integrate"
              " AI models into your applications...",
        max_sentences="2",
    )
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())
Prompt templates support nested function calls:
can appear inside another prompt, allowing compositional prompts without writing Python glue code.
Native Functions — Python Code as Skills
decorator provide the metadata SK needs to auto-describe the function to planners and agents.

from semantic_kernel.functions import kernel_function
from semantic_kernel.kernel_pydantic import KernelBaseModel
import httpx

class WeatherPlugin:
    @kernel_function(
        name="GetCurrentWeather",
        description="Return the current weather for a city.",
    )
    def get_weather(self, city: str) -> str:
        # Real implementation would call a weather API
        return f"It is 22°C and sunny in {city}."

kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="Weather")

async def main():
    result = await kernel.invoke(
        kernel.plugins["Weather"]["GetCurrentWeather"],
        city="London",
    )
    print(result)   # "It is 22°C and sunny in London."

asyncio.run(main())
Building a Plugin from a Class
methods in a class. Register the class once with
. All decorated methods become individually callable functions under that plugin name.
— a typed list of messages. Add
entries, then call the chat service directly or let a

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai.prompt_execution_settings.open_ai_prompt_execution_settings import (
    OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings,
)
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

kernel = Kernel()
service = OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o-mini")
kernel.add_service(service)

chat_history = ChatHistory()
chat_history.add_system_message(
    "You are a helpful financial analyst assistant."
)

async def chat(user_msg: str):
    chat_history.add_user_message(user_msg)
    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(temperature=0.0)

    response = await service.get_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=chat_history,
        settings=settings,
        kernel=kernel,
    )
    chat_history.add_assistant_message(str(response))
    return str(response)

async def main():
    print(await chat("What is EBITDA?"))
    print(await chat("Can you give an example?"))

asyncio.run(main())
. The method returns an async generator of partial content chunks.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai.prompt_execution_settings.open_ai_prompt_execution_settings import (
    OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings,
)

async def stream_chat(kernel, service, user_msg: str):
    history = ChatHistory()
    history.add_system_message("You are a concise assistant.")
    history.add_user_message(user_msg)
    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(temperature=0)

    full_response = ""
    async for chunk in service.get_streaming_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=history,
        settings=settings,
        kernel=kernel,
    ):
        if chunk.content:
            print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)
            full_response += chunk.content
    print()
    return full_response

asyncio.run(stream_chat(kernel, service, "Explain RAG in 3 sentences."))
A planner reads the available plugins and functions, then uses an LLM to generate a multi-step plan to achieve a goal. Semantic Kernel ships two planners:
the recommended modern approach — lets the LLM call functions as tools, in any order, as many times as needed (equivalent to a ReAct-style tool-calling agent)
the LLM generates a Handlebars template plan upfront, which is then executed deterministically — good when you need an auditable plan before execution

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai.prompt_execution_settings.open_ai_prompt_execution_settings import (
    OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings,
)
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

kernel = Kernel()
service = OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o")
kernel.add_service(service)

# Register plugins
kernel.add_plugin(FinancePlugin(), plugin_name="Finance")
kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="Weather")

async def agent_chat(user_msg: str):
    history = ChatHistory()
    history.add_user_message(user_msg)

    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
        # Allow the LLM to call any registered function
        function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),
    )

    response = await service.get_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=history,
        settings=settings,
        kernel=kernel,
    )
    return str(response)

# The LLM will call Finance.GetStockPrice and WeatherPlugin.GetCurrentWeather
# automatically when needed
result = asyncio.run(agent_chat(
    "What is Microsoft's stock price and what is the weather in Redmond?"
))
print(result)
SK's memory abstraction wraps a vector store behind a simple
to embed and store a string; call
to retrieve the most semantically similar stored facts. Any vector store with a SK connector can back it.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import (
    OpenAIChatCompletion,
    OpenAITextEmbedding,
)
from semantic_kernel.core_plugins import TextMemoryPlugin
from semantic_kernel.memory import SemanticTextMemory
from semantic_kernel.connectors.memory.chroma import ChromaMemoryStore

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o-mini"))

embed_service = OpenAITextEmbedding(
    service_id="embed",
    ai_model_id="text-embedding-3-small",
)
kernel.add_service(embed_service)

memory_store = ChromaMemoryStore(persist_directory="./chroma_memory")
memory = SemanticTextMemory(storage=memory_store, embeddings_generator=embed_service)
kernel.add_plugin(TextMemoryPlugin(memory), plugin_name="Memory")

async def main():
    # Store facts
    await memory.save_information(
        collection="facts",
        id="fact1",
        text="The Paris Agreement was signed in 2015 to address climate change.",
    )
    await memory.save_information(
        collection="facts",
        id="fact2",
        text="Renewable energy now accounts for over 30% of global electricity generation.",
    )

    # Recall
    results = await memory.search("facts", "climate agreements", limit=2)
    for r in results:
        print(f"Relevance: {r.relevance:.3f} — {r.text[:80]}")

asyncio.run(main())
interface provides before/after hooks around every function call. Use them for logging, caching, PII scrubbing, rate limiting, or injecting context.

from semantic_kernel.filters import FunctionInvocationContext
from typing import Callable, Awaitable

class LoggingFilter:
    async def on_function_invocation(
        self,
        context: FunctionInvocationContext,
        next: Callable[[FunctionInvocationContext], Awaitable[None]],
    ) -> None:
        fn = context.function.fully_qualified_name
        print(f"[PRE ] Calling {fn} with args={context.arguments}")
        await next(context)   # run the actual function
        print(f"[POST] {fn} returned: {context.result}")

kernel.add_filter(LoggingFilter())

# All subsequent kernel.invoke() calls will print before and after
Switching from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI requires only changing the service class. The rest of the kernel, plugins, and planners stay identical.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import AzureChatCompletion

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(
    AzureChatCompletion(
        service_id="azure_chat",
        deployment_name="gpt-4o",
        endpoint="https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/",
        # api_key read from AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY env by default
        # Or use DefaultAzureCredential for managed identity
    )
)
Semantic Kernel vs LangChain vs LlamaIndex
Microsoft stack, enterprise Copilot, multi-language teams
Rapid prototyping, broad ecosystem, agent chains
Yes — first-class Azure OpenAI, AI Search

your organisation is on Azure, needs C# or Java support, or is building Copilot plugins. The plugin contract maps directly to Microsoft's Copilot Studio plugin format. For pure Python RAG workflows, LlamaIndex has richer retrieval primitives. For fast prototyping with a wide ecosystem, LangChain has the most integrations.
